text: The following video depicts the repair of a boctelic hernia in an adult. This is performed with the da Vinci robotic system using a standard two-arm technique and the high-definition camera. This is the most common type of congenital diaphragmatic hernia and occurs in approximately one in two thousand five hundred births and is more common in men than in women. This defect is typically on the left and occurs as a result of failure of the pleural peritoneal canal closure separating the abdominal from the thoracic cavities.

The surgeon identifies a small posterior lateral hernia with an intact hernia sac. Using hook monopolar cautery, the hernia sac is dissected from the surrounding diaphragm. Properitoneal fat is excised to reduce the hernia mass and prevent incarceration, then retrieved with an endo-catch bag through the assistance port. This provides clear visualization of the defect edges, allowing reduction of hernia sac contents into the abdominal cavity and preparation of a clean diaphragm edge for repair.

A GORE-TEX dual mesh patch is fashioned to repair the diaphragmatic defect. It is secured laterally to the thoracic wall using Ethibond sutures and a tie knot system. A running non-absorbable suture is used to secure the mesh circumferentially through the diaphragmatic defect, recreating the separation between the abdominal and thoracic cavities. The redundant patch material is trimmed with endoshears for a tight fit. A second stitch is initiated posteriorly and brought around to meet the anterior portion of the repair, while the lateral wall is reinforced with a third running suture.
The patient had an uneventful recovery and remains asymptomatic to date.
